---
title: "Forward-Pass Adaptation of Frozen Language Models by Sparse Signed Log-Gates"
subtitle: "A framework-independent specification of the method"
date: 2026-06-11
abstract: |
  This document specifies, in mathematical and algorithmic terms only, a family
  of methods for adapting a frozen autoregressive language model without
  modifying any of its weights. The core object is a sparse *signed log-gate
  controller*: a small set of bounded scalar gates that multiplicatively
  rescale selected hidden-state channels, $h' = e^{s}h$. Around it sit five
  methods: (1) saliency-guided selection and gradient training of the gates;
  (2) a measurable criterion — the *activation-control NTK duality test* — for
  whether a gate update can realize the effect of a full-weight SGD step;
  (3) an exact composition algebra for combining independently trained
  controllers; (4) a retrieval-weighted scheme that turns controllers into
  persistent behavioral memories; and (5) an order-sensitivity score for
  evidence-support verification. Every quantity is defined precisely enough to
  reimplement in any framework or language; no reference is made to any
  particular software implementation.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  enabled: false
---

# Setting and notation {#sec-setting}

Let $f_\theta$ be a trained autoregressive (causal) language model with frozen
parameters $\theta$, vocabulary size $V$, a stack of $L$ decoder blocks, and
hidden width $C$. For an input token sequence, write
$h_{\ell,t,c} \in \mathbb{R}$ for channel $c$ of the hidden-state vector at
token position $t$ associated with block $\ell$, measured at one of two
*intervention sites*:

- **block output**: the hidden-state vector emitted by block $\ell$ into the
  residual stream (default), or
- **block input**: the hidden-state vector entering block $\ell$.

A training example is a pair (prompt, completion). The example is tokenized as
the concatenation of the two; prompt positions are unsupervised, completion
positions are supervised (teacher forcing). Define the *task loss* on a batch
as the mean next-token cross-entropy over supervised positions only:

$$
L \;=\; \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}|}\sum_{(b,t)\in\mathcal{T}}
-\log p_\theta\!\left(y_{b,t+1}\mid y_{b,\le t}\right),
$$

where $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of supervised (batch, position) pairs. All
NLL ("negative log-likelihood") numbers in this document are token-weighted
means of this quantity; *token accuracy* is the fraction of supervised
positions where the argmax next-token prediction equals the teacher token.

Throughout, "the base model" means $f_\theta$ with no intervention attached.
A standing invariant of every method below is that **$\theta$ is never
permanently modified**: every intervention is a removable transformation of
hidden states, and the one procedure that does touch weights (the duality
target measurement, @sec-target) applies a step and exactly reverses it.

# The signed log-gate controller {#sec-controller}

## Intervention

A *controller* is a sparse set of $k$ gates. Gate $j$ addresses one pair
$(\ell_j, c_j)$ of (block, channel), with all pairs distinct, and carries a
bounded signed log-value $s_j$. When the controller is *attached*, the hidden
state at the chosen site is rescaled, identically at every token position:

$$
h'_{\ell_j,\,t,\,c_j} \;=\; e^{s_j}\, h_{\ell_j,\,t,\,c_j},
\qquad |s_j| \le s_{\max} .
\tag{1}
$$

Unaddressed channels are untouched. The intervention is multiplicative in
activation space and therefore *additive in log space*, which is what makes
the composition algebra of @sec-composition exact. With the reference bound
$s_{\max}=0.05$, every channel scale lies in
$e^{\pm 0.05}\approx[0.951,\,1.051]$.

## Parameterization

The trainable parameter is an unconstrained vector $r\in\mathbb{R}^k$; the
applied gate values are the smooth bounded reparameterization

$$
s_j \;=\; s_{\max}\tanh(r_j).
\tag{2}
$$

This keeps every gate strictly inside $(-s_{\max}, s_{\max})$ by construction,
so unconstrained first-order optimizers need no projection step. The inverse
map, needed when writing externally computed gate values back into the
parameterization, is

$$
r_j \;=\; \operatorname{atanh}\!\big(\operatorname{clip}(s_j/s_{\max},\,
-1+\varepsilon_r,\; 1-\varepsilon_r)\big),
\qquad \varepsilon_r = 10^{-6}.
\tag{3}
$$

Whenever a gate-value vector is assigned from outside (composition, pathwise
updates, deserialization), it is first clipped to $[-s_{\max}, s_{\max}]$ and
then encoded through (3). The controller is initialized at $r=0$, i.e. the
identity intervention.

## Gate selection by first-order saliency {#sec-selection}

Gates are selected before any training, from the *frozen, unmodified* model.
The key identity: for a multiplicative log-gate inserted at $s=0$, the chain
rule gives the loss sensitivity directly from one ordinary backward pass,

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial s_{\ell,c}}\bigg|_{s=0}
\;=\;\sum_{(b,t)} \frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{\ell,b,t,c}}\;
h_{\ell,b,t,c}.
\tag{4}
$$

The selection statistic deliberately replaces the magnitude of this sum by the
sum of per-position magnitudes,

$$
\sigma_{\ell,c} \;=\; \sum_{\text{batches}}\;\sum_{(b,t)}
\left|\,\frac{\partial L}{\partial h_{\ell,b,t,c}}\; h_{\ell,b,t,c}\right|,
\tag{5}
$$

an upper bound on $|\partial L/\partial s_{\ell,c}|$ that cannot miss a
channel whose positive and negative token contributions cancel. The procedure:

1. Fix a candidate block set $\mathcal{L}\subseteq\{0,\dots,L-1\}$ (default:
   all blocks).
2. For each of up to $N_{\mathrm{sel}}$ training batches (reference
   $N_{\mathrm{sel}}=16$, batch size 8): run a forward pass capturing
   $h_{\ell,\cdot,\cdot,\cdot}$ at the intervention site for every
   $\ell\in\mathcal{L}$; backpropagate the task loss to those hidden states;
   accumulate (5).
3. Select the top $k$ (block, channel) pairs by $\sigma_{\ell,c}$, jointly
   across all candidate blocks (reference $k = 5000$).

The hidden states need a gradient source even though all weights are frozen;
any single differentiable leaf upstream of every block suffices (e.g. the
input embedding), and is restored to its frozen state afterwards.

## Training objective {#sec-training}

With the controller attached, minimize over $r$ only:

$$
L_{\mathrm{total}} \;=\; L_{\mathrm{task}}
\;+\;\lambda_{\mathrm{ret}}\, L_{\mathrm{ret}}
\;+\;\lambda_{\mathrm{KL}}\,
\mathrm{KL}\!\left(P_{\mathrm{base}}\,\Vert\,P_{\mathrm{ctrl}}\right)
\;+\;\lambda_{2}\,\frac{1}{k}\sum_j s_j^2 ,
\tag{6}
$$

where

- $L_{\mathrm{task}}$ is the supervised cross-entropy on the adaptation set;
- $L_{\mathrm{ret}}$ is the same loss on an optional *retain* set, protecting
  behavior the adaptation must not erase;
- the KL term is the mean, over supervised retain positions, of
  $\mathrm{KL}$ from the base model's next-token distribution to the
  gated model's distribution at the same position — the base distribution is
  computed with the intervention *temporarily removed*, so the regularizer
  compares the same weights with and without gates;
- the final term is a mean-square penalty on the applied gate values $s$
  (not on $r$).

Both $\lambda_{\mathrm{ret}}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{KL}}$ default to zero and
require an explicit retain set when nonzero.

Optimization is by an Adam-type first-order method with decoupled weight decay
applied to $r$ alone (reference: $T=240$ steps, learning rate
$5\times10^{-3}$, weight decay 0, batch size 8, minibatches visited in a
fixed cyclic order: step $i$ uses batch $i \bmod n_{\mathrm{batches}}$).
If a validation set is supplied, evaluate validation NLL every
$V$ steps, where the default interval is $V=\max(1, \min(50, \lfloor T/10
\rfloor))$ and always at the final step; keep the parameters that achieved
the best validation NLL; optionally stop early after a fixed number of
consecutive non-improving evaluations.

## Artifact contract

A trained controller is fully described by: the intervention site; the block
count $L$ and hidden width $C$ it was built against; the index lists
$\{\ell_j\}, \{c_j\}$; the parameter vector $r$ (or equivalently $s$); the
bound $s_{\max}$; and the *identity* of the model and tokenizer it was trained
on (name and exact revision). A controller must be refused at load time if any
structural field disagrees with the live model, and by default if the identity
disagrees. Validity requires: distinct (block, channel) keys, indices in
range, finite values, $s_{\max}$ finite and positive. This contract is part of
the method: a controller is a *measurement-bound* object, meaningful only
against the exact network it was fitted to.

## Reference hyperparameters

| Quantity | Symbol | Reference value |
|---|---|---:|
| gate budget | $k$ | 5000 |
| gate bound | $s_{\max}$ | 0.05 |
| candidate blocks | $\mathcal{L}$ | all |
| intervention site | — | block output |
| selection batches | $N_{\mathrm{sel}}$ | 16 |
| training steps | $T$ | 240 |
| learning rate | — | $5\times10^{-3}$ |
| batch size | — | 8 |
| mean-$s^2$ penalty | $\lambda_2$ | $10^{-5}$ |
| retain / KL weights | $\lambda_{\mathrm{ret}}, \lambda_{\mathrm{KL}}$ | 0 (opt-in) |

# Activation-control NTK duality {#sec-dual}

The theory-facing question is sharper than "do gates help?": **can a gate
update reproduce, on a chosen observable, the first-order effect of a
full-weight SGD step on the frozen model?** When it can, the controller is
*locally dual* to weight-space fine-tuning on that observable. The method
answers this with a measured residual, not an assumption, and explicitly
retires two tempting but invalid certificates: a small gate norm, and
linearity of the trained controller from its initialization (the secant
statement of @sec-secant). The only admissible certificate is the range test
below.

## Projected logit coordinates {#sec-projection}

All fields are compared in a fixed finite-dimensional projection of the
model's supervised next-token logits, built once from a *baseline* forward
pass on a calibration batch and then held fixed.

Let $P$ be the number of supervised next-token positions in the calibration
batch and $z_p\in\mathbb{R}^V$ the logit vector at position $p$. Each
position's logits are first centered across the vocabulary,
$\tilde z_p = z_p - \overline{z_p}$, so the coordinates measure distribution
shape rather than a constant offset. Three projection modes:

- **target** — one coordinate per position: the teacher-token logit
  $\tilde z_p[y_p]$. Dimension $P$. Cheap, but tests only the teacher-token
  direction.
- **top-$k$** — per position, the teacher token plus the $k$ highest-scoring
  *non-teacher* tokens of the **baseline** pass (the teacher token is excluded
  from the top-$k$ before concatenation so it is never duplicated and never
  double-weighted). The gathered $(k{+}1)$-vector is additionally re-centered
  within the event set. Dimension $P(k{+}1)$. Reference $k=32$; the practical
  default.
- **full** — all $V$ coordinates per position. Dimension $PV$; exact but
  large, to be guarded by an explicit dimension cap.

The token index sets are frozen from the baseline pass; subsequent evaluations
of the projection (under gates, under stepped weights) gather the same
coordinates. Write $F(s)\in\mathbb{R}^D$ for the projected logits as a
function of the applied gate values $s$, with weights fixed; and
$F_\theta$ for the same map as a function of weights with gates fixed.

## The measured target field {#sec-target}

For support-loss gradient $g_S=\nabla_\theta L_S$ and learning rate $\eta$,
first-order (NTK) reasoning says a full-weight SGD step displaces the
projected calibration logits by

$$
d \;=\; -\,\eta\, J_{\theta,C}\, g_S
\;=\; -\,\eta\, J_{\theta,C}\, J_{\theta,S}^{\top}\, g_S^{(\mathrm{out})},
\tag{7}
$$

where $J_{\theta,C} = \partial F_\theta/\partial\theta$ on the calibration
projection. The method does **not** trust (7) symbolically. It measures the
displacement by actually performing a small reversible step:

1. Choose the differentiated parameter subset $\Theta'$ (all parameters, or a
   name-filtered subset such as the attention projections; a hard cap on the
   total differentiated parameter count is recommended as a cost guard).
2. With the controller attached at its current state, compute
   $g=\nabla_{\Theta'} L_S$ on one support batch.
3. Record $d_{\mathrm{base}}=F(\theta)$; apply $\theta\leftarrow\theta-\eta g$
   in place; record $d_{\mathrm{after}}=F(\theta-\eta g)$; **restore the
   weights exactly** by adding $\eta g$ back, with the restoration guaranteed
   to run even if the intermediate evaluation fails.
4. The target field is $d = d_{\mathrm{after}} - d_{\mathrm{base}}$.

Reference step size $\eta = 10^{-5}$. Record alongside $d$: $\lVert g\rVert$,
$\eta\lVert g\rVert$, $\lVert d\rVert$, and $L_S$. The step size must be swept
in practice: too large and $d$ contains a nonlinear weight move; too small and
$d$ drowns in numerical noise.

## The gate tangent and the metric {#sec-tangent}

At controller state $s_t$, define the *gate tangent*

$$
B \;=\; B(s_t) \;=\; \frac{\partial F(s)}{\partial s}\bigg|_{s=s_t}
\;\in\;\mathbb{R}^{D\times k}.
\tag{8}
$$

$B$ is never materialized. Two matrix-free primitives are required, both as
exact derivatives through the same differentiable computation of $F(s)$:

- $Bv$: the forward-mode directional derivative of $F$ at $s_t$ along $v$;
- $B^\top y$: the reverse-mode gradient of $\langle F(s), y\rangle$ at $s_t$.

Mixing an exact reverse-mode $B^\top$ with a finite-difference forward
$B$ destroys the adjoint relation $\langle Bv,y\rangle=\langle v,B^\top
y\rangle$ and with it the validity of the solver below; finite-difference
$Bv$ is admissible only as an explicitly labeled ablation whose residuals are
not evidence.

Gate displacements are measured in a diagonal metric $M\succ 0$:

- **identity** $M=I$ — an ablation, not a claim about natural units;
- **activation** — gate displacement measured in units of induced activation
  displacement. For a log-gate, $\delta h_{c} = e^{s_c} h_c\,\delta s_c$, so

$$
M_j \;\approx\; \mathbb{E}\big[h_{\ell_j,\cdot,c_j}^2\big]\; e^{2 s_j},
\tag{9}
$$

  with the expectation over batch and token positions of the calibration
  batch at gate $j$'s site. Preferred estimator: record activations *with the
  current controller attached*, which folds the $e^{2s_j}$ factor in
  empirically and lets upstream gates influence downstream metrics; the
  closed-form variant records base activations and applies $e^{2s_j}$
  explicitly. Floor every entry at $\varepsilon_M=10^{-6}$.

## The ridge-projected solve {#sec-solve}

The duality question — does $d$ lie in $\operatorname{range}(B)$? — is
answered by the metric ridge projection

$$
\big(B M^{-1} B^{\top} + \lambda I\big)\,\alpha \;=\; d,
\qquad
u \;=\; M^{-1} B^{\top}\alpha,
\tag{10}
$$

solved by conjugate gradients on the $D$-dimensional system using only the
two matrix-free primitives (reference: ridge $\lambda=10^{-4}$, at most 16 CG
iterations, relative residual tolerance $10^{-5}$). The operator
$K=BM^{-1}B^\top+\lambda I$ is symmetric positive-definite exactly when the
two primitives are mutually adjoint — which is *verified, not assumed*:

- **adjoint probe**: draw random unit vectors $v\in\mathbb{R}^k$,
  $y\in\mathbb{R}^D$; report the relative gap
  $\dfrac{|\langle Bv,y\rangle-\langle v,B^{\top}y\rangle|}
        {|\langle Bv,y\rangle|+|\langle v,B^{\top}y\rangle|}$;
- **symmetry probe**: draw random unit $a,b\in\mathbb{R}^D$; report the same
  relative gap between $a^{\top}Kb$ and $b^{\top}Ka$.

If either probe is large, the solver is invalid and no residual from it means
anything.

## Residual taxonomy {#sec-residuals}

The verdict is deliberately not one number. With $u$ from (10) and
$\operatorname{clip}(\cdot)$ the elementwise box clip to
$[-s_{\max},s_{\max}]$:

| Diagnostic | Definition | High value means |
|---|---|---|
| adjoint error | probe gap above | solver invalid |
| symmetry error | probe gap above | solver invalid |
| CG convergence | iterations, residual history, converged flag | under-solve mimics range failure |
| **range residual** | $\lVert d - Bu\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$ | target not in the local gate-tangent range — **the duality verdict** |
| realized residual | $\lVert d - (F(s_t{+}u)-F(s_t))\rVert / \lVert d\rVert$ | range fine but the finite step leaves the linear regime |
| clipped realized residual | same with $s_t{+}u$ replaced by $\operatorname{clip}(s_t{+}u)$ | what the *deployable* (bounded) update actually realizes |
| box clip fraction | fraction of entries of $s_t{+}u$ changed by clipping (tolerance $10^{-7}$) | solved step ≠ applied step |
| box active fraction | fraction of entries of the clipped state at the bound | budget saturated |
| clip-update residual | $\lVert u - u_{\mathrm{applied}}\rVert/\lVert u\rVert$ | how much clipping changed the step |

Cosines between $d$ and each realized field, and the norms of all fields and
of $u$ (Euclidean and metric), are reported alongside. In a regime where the
solve is essentially exact (small problems, full CG convergence), reference
clean-instrument tolerances are: adjoint and symmetry errors $<10^{-5}$,
range residual $<10^{-4}$, realized residual $<5\times10^{-3}$; and the
clipped realized residual is never below the unconstrained one.

The residual *ordering* identifies the culprit:

```text
adjoint or symmetry error high?
└─ yes → instrument invalid (mixed-mode derivatives or a bug); fix first.
└─ no → CG unconverged?
    └─ yes → raise the iteration cap; an under-solve mimics range failure.
    └─ no → range residual high?
        └─ yes → genuine range failure on this projection.
                 (Check the projection is not too narrow: "target" can
                 pass while "top-k"/"full" refute.)
        └─ no → realized residual high?
            └─ yes → realization failure: step too large or chart too
                     curved; sweep the target step size η.
            └─ no → box clip fraction high?
                └─ yes → read the clipped realized residual; the applied
                         step is not the solved step.
                └─ no → local duality holds on this projection.
```

## The secant diagnostic {#sec-secant}

A separate, weaker instrument answers a different question: *is the trained
controller still explained by its initialization tangent?* For a fitted gate
vector $s$, estimate the initial tangent along the ray through the origin by
the central difference $t=(F(\epsilon s)-F(-\epsilon s))/(2\epsilon)$ with
$\epsilon=10^{-3}$, and for $\alpha\in\{0.125,0.25,0.5,0.75,1.0\}$ report

$$
e(\alpha)=\frac{\lVert (F(\alpha s)-F(0)) - \alpha\,t\rVert}
               {\lVert F(\alpha s)-F(0)\rVert},
\qquad
\cos\big(F(\alpha s)-F(0),\;\alpha t\big),
$$

plus, at interior points $\alpha\in\{0.0625,\dots,0.875\}$, the cosine
between the *local* central-difference derivative along the ray and the
initial tangent $t$ ("tangent drift").

**Interpretation rule (important):** a high secant error at $\alpha=1$ says
only that the initial linearization is not a global model of the trained
controller. It does **not** refute duality: every local field solve along the
training path can succeed while the endpoint has left the initial tangent.
The range residual of @sec-residuals is the duality test; the secant is a
diagnostic of the (retired) global-linearity story.

## Pathwise field-locked fitting {#sec-fitdual}

The constructive counterpart of the diagnostic: instead of minimizing the
support loss directly, repeatedly steer the gates along the measured
full-weight field.

```text
initialize gates by saliency selection (s = 0)            # @sec-selection
for step = 1..N:                                          # reference N = 8
    take the next minibatch (cyclically); use it as both
        support and calibration batch
    build the logit projection at the current state        # @sec-projection
    measure the target field d by the reversible
        weight step                                        # @sec-target
    solve (10) for the gate update u                       # @sec-solve
    s ← clip(s + γ·u)   into [-s_max, s_max]               # reference γ = 1
    record the full residual taxonomy, plus the realized
        clip fraction and clip-update residual of this step
```

Each step re-linearizes both the target and the tangent at the current state,
so the procedure tracks the weight-SGD field *pathwise*; per-step clipped
residuals quantify exactly where the bounded gate basis stopped being able to
follow it.

# Composition algebra {#sec-composition}

## Rule

Because the intervention (1) multiplies channel scales, controllers compose
by **addition of signed log-gate values on the union of their supports**:

$$
s^{(AB)}_{\ell,c} \;=\; \operatorname{clip}\!\Big(
\sum_i w_i\, s^{(i)}_{\ell,c},\;
-s_{\max}^{\star},\, +s_{\max}^{\star}\Big),
\tag{11}
$$

where $s^{(i)}_{\ell,c}=0$ off controller $i$'s support, $w_i$ are scalar
composition weights (default 1), and $s_{\max}^{\star}$ defaults to the
largest bound among the inputs. The result is re-encoded through (3) as an
ordinary controller. Absent clipping, attaching the composition is *exactly*
equivalent to attaching all inputs simultaneously — this is the sense in
which the algebra is exact, and why all interference risk concentrates in
the clip.

## Preconditions

Composition must be **refused**, not coerced, unless all inputs agree on:
hidden width $C$, block count $L$, intervention site, and the controller
formulation version. Model/tokenizer identity must agree on an
*all-or-none* basis: if any input declares an identity, all must declare the
same one — a state with known identity may not be composed with one whose
identity is unknown. The composition records its provenance (input count,
weights, input gate counts).

## Interference statistics and pre-flight thresholds

For each input pair $(a,b)$, with sparse gate maps $g_a, g_b$ and dense
vectors $v_a, v_b \in \mathbb{R}^{LC}$:

- support overlap and union; Jaccard index;
- **opposing-sign fraction**: among shared keys, the fraction with
  $g_a\,g_b<0$;
- $\lVert v_a\rVert$, $\lVert v_b\rVert$, $\langle v_a,v_b\rangle$, cosine.

A conservative pre-flight check on a proposed composition raises a warning
when (reference thresholds):

| Signal | Trigger | Mechanical risk |
|---|---|---|
| saturation | $>2\%$ of union gates exceed $s_{\max}^{\star}$ *before* clipping | the sum is not what gets applied |
| opposing overlap | $>50\%$ of any pair's shared gates have opposite signs | tasks fight over channels |
| antagonistic direction | any pairwise cosine $< -0.1$ | globally opposed controllers |

These are mechanical risk signals only; they do not certify behavioral
compatibility. The decisive test is always behavioral: the composition must
retain each constituent task's held-out improvement (@sec-protocol).
Remedies, in order of preference: asymmetric weights $w_i<1$, a smaller
$s_{\max}$, or a larger gate budget per task — *not* a larger clip bound.

# Controllers as persistent behavioral memory {#sec-memory}

## Model

A *memory item* is a controller fitted to one unit of adaptation (a document,
a user preference, a procedure, a task style) together with retrieval text (a
short descriptor, optionally augmented with snippets of the item's training
prompts) and tags. At inference time: score all items against the query,
take the top-$k$ hits above a threshold, convert scores to composition
weights, compose by (11), attach, generate. This trades prompt tokens for
gate state: the forward pass is biased toward the remembered behavior without
inserting any text into the context.

## Retrieval scoring

Three scorers over the items' retrieval text:

- **lexical** (dependency-free baseline): tokenize to lowercase word tokens
  with stop-words removed; with $n$ items and document frequency
  $\mathrm{df}(t)$,
  $$
  \mathrm{idf}(t)=\ln\frac{1+n}{1+\mathrm{df}(t)}+1,
  $$
  build tf–idf vectors (term count × idf) for the query and each item; score
  by cosine.
- **embedding**: cosine between $\ell_2$-normalized text embeddings of query
  and item from any sentence-embedding model.
- **hybrid** (preferred): min–max normalize each scorer's values to $[0,1]$
  *within the current candidate set*, then mix
  $$
  \mathrm{score} = \alpha\,\widehat{\mathrm{emb}} +
  (1-\alpha)\,\widehat{\mathrm{lex}},\qquad \alpha=0.65 .
  $$
  Degenerate-normalization rule: if all candidates tie, map them all to $1$
  if the tied value is positive and to $0$ otherwise — otherwise a single
  clearly matching item would be normalized to zero and spuriously dropped.

## Weighting and the no-hit fallback

Hits below a strictly positive minimum score (reference $10^{-8}$) are
discarded; **if no hit survives, no controller is attached and the base model
answers** — a zero-signal query must never pull in an arbitrary memory.
Surviving top-$k$ scores $c_1..c_m$ become composition weights by softmax
with temperature $\tau=0.25$:

$$
w_i=\frac{e^{c_i/\tau}}{\sum_j e^{c_j/\tau}},
$$

(alternatives: score-proportional, uniform), so composing several memories
interpolates rather than sums uncontrolledly. Composition then follows
@sec-composition unchanged.

## Scope boundary and trust

Controller memories steer *behavior* — procedures, style, preferences, local
terminology. They cannot insert missing strings into the context: if the
answer requires a quote, a date, or a citation, the evidence belongs in the
prompt (retrieval-augmented generation), not in a controller. And because a
controller is executable-adjacent model state, a memory store is a trust
boundary: items must be bound to the exact model/tokenizer identity, kept
versioned and deletable, and audited — a stale, poisoned, or mismatched
controller degrades the model silently.

# Order-sensitivity verification (ISR) {#sec-isr}

## Hypothesis

A verifier's support probability for a *well-supported* claim should be
stable under permutations of the evidence presentation order; instability
signals that the verifier keys on position rather than content. Penalizing
dispersion across orderings should therefore improve hallucination detection.

## Verifier

Given evidence spans $(e_1,\dots,e_n)$ and a claim, the verifier input is the
fixed template

```text
Evidence:
- <span 1>
- <span 2>
...
Claim: <claim>
Is the claim fully supported by the evidence? Answer YES or NO:
```

with two answer continuations, `" YES"` and `" NO"` (each with a leading
space). The verifier score is

$$
q \;=\; P(\mathrm{YES}) \;=\;
\operatorname{softmax}\big(\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{YES}},\,
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{NO}}\big)_1,
$$

where $\mathcal{L}_c$ is the sum of token log-probabilities of continuation
$c$ appended after the template (computed in high precision). When the two
continuations tokenize to different lengths, divide each $\mathcal{L}_c$ by
its token count before the softmax (length normalization, opt-in).

Batching note: scoring all (ordering, continuation) branches in one
right-padded batch is admissible for a causal model with an attention mask
because the logits at real positions are provably unchanged — an optimization
is acceptable only when it provably does not move the measurement.

## Orderings and scores

For each row, with $m$ orderings (reference $m=6$): the first is the
canonical (given) order; the remaining $m-1$ are uniform random permutations
drawn from a deterministic per-row generator seeded by
$\mathrm{seed}\cdot 100003 + \mathrm{row\ index}$, so every score is exactly
reproducible and shards can be scored independently. With per-ordering
probabilities $q_1,\dots,q_m$:

$$
q_{\mathrm{canon}}=q_1,\qquad
q_{\mathrm{marg}}=\frac1m\sum_i q_i,\qquad
\mathrm{ISR}=q_{\mathrm{marg}}-\beta\,\sigma(q),
\tag{12}
$$

with $\sigma$ the population standard deviation and dispersion penalty
$\beta=1$. $q_{\mathrm{marg}}$ is the headline ranking score;
$\mathrm{ISR}$ is the order-stability-gated score. Known defeat: a
*confidently wrong* verifier (low dispersion, wrong answer) passes the
stability gate — ISR is a verifier-quality amplifier, not a factuality proof.

## Evaluation metrics

Scores are evaluated as rankers of supported ($y=1$) vs unsupported ($y=0$)
rows.

- **AUROC**, tie-aware (Mann–Whitney with average ranks): with average ranks
  $R_i$ of all scores,
  $$
  \mathrm{AUROC}=\frac{\sum_{i:y_i=1} R_i - n_+(n_++1)/2}{n_+\,n_-}.
  $$
- **TPR at a false-positive budget** ("leak", reference $0.1$): sort the
  $n_-$ negative scores ascending; the threshold is the element at index
  $\lceil (1-\mathrm{leak})\,n_-\rceil - 1$ (clamped to a valid index;
  leak $=0$ uses the maximum negative score); report the fraction of positive
  scores *strictly above* the threshold.

Rows that fail to score are recorded as errors and excluded rather than
aborting the run; sharding over rows is by index modulo shard count; raw
claim/evidence text is excluded from results by default (privacy).

## Optional closed-form order-debias on the value cache

A fourth score reuses the linearize-and-ridge-project move of @sec-solve on a
different control surface: the attention *value cache* of the first few
blocks (reference: 4), with the canonical-order input prefilled. Let
$z_0\in\mathbb{R}^2$ be the *centered* (YES, NO) event logits, and let
$q\mapsto z(q)$ be the event logits as a function of an additive delta $q$ on
the chosen cache coordinates. Compute the Jacobian
$B=\partial z/\partial q|_{q=0}\in\mathbb{R}^{2\times\dim(q)}$ by two
reverse-mode passes; set the steering target

$$
d \;=\; \mathrm{clp}\big([\,\bar q,\;1-\bar q\,]\big)-z_0,
$$

where $\bar q = q_{\mathrm{marg}}$ and $\mathrm{clp}(p)$ is the centered
logarithm of the probability vector $p$ clamped to
$[\varepsilon,1-\varepsilon]$, $\varepsilon=10^{-6}$, and renormalized. Solve
the ridge problem in closed form (in high precision):

$$
\min_q\ \lVert Bq-d\rVert^2+\rho\lVert q\rVert^2
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\alpha=(BB^{\top}+\rho I)^{-1}d,\quad q=B^{\top}\alpha,
\tag{13}
$$

with $\rho=10^{-3}$; rescale $q$ to norm at most a fixed budget (reference
10) if it exceeds it, recording that clipping occurred; re-evaluate the event
logits with the delta applied and report
$q_{\mathrm{kv}}=\operatorname{softmax}(z(q))_{\mathrm{YES}}$, together with
the delta norm, the clip flag, and the solve dimensions. Single-span rows are
skipped; a failure of this score on a row must not invalidate the row's other
three scores.

# Experimental protocol and refutation controls {#sec-protocol}

The methods above come with a fixed evaluation discipline. Every positive
claim is paired with the control that would refute it.

## Reporting requirements

For any adaptation claim, report on the **same** train/eval manifest, with
pinned model and tokenizer revisions and dataset hashes:

- the base model (floor, and the contamination check: if the base already
  solves the eval, deltas are washed out);
- the controller, across a **capacity sweep** of the gate budget $k$ — a
  512-gate and a 10 000-gate controller are different objects;
- a weight-space baseline (e.g. low-rank adaptation or SFT) at matched
  budget;
- a **random-gate control**: same budget, same training, gates chosen
  uniformly at random — the direct refutation of the saliency-selection
  hypothesis (@sec-selection);
- both teacher-forced NLL **and** exact long-horizon answer accuracy: NLL can
  improve while exact accuracy does not, and a result reported on only one is
  incomplete;
- for multiple-choice tasks: length-normalized choice NLL as the headline,
  with summed-loss accuracy disclosed alongside so length bias stays visible;
- adaptation wall-clock time and peak memory for system claims.

## Memory design matrix

For the persistent-memory claim, evaluate per task the five conditions:

| Condition | Isolates |
|---|---|
| base | floor / contamination |
| gold memory (retrieval oracle-ed away) | controller quality |
| retrieval from task descriptors | retrieval text quality (diagnostic) |
| per-example query retrieval | the realistic path |
| all memories composed | interference cost |

Adjacent differences separate the three failure axes: base→gold isolates the
controller, gold→retrieved isolates retrieval, retrieved→composed isolates
composition. Emit one artifact per failure hypothesis: every memory scored on
every task (wrong-memory harm and selectivity margins); retrieval recall at
$k=1..5$ for each scorer separately (a lexical-descriptor failure is not a
controller failure); pairwise gate geometry and a composition report;
per-controller saturation statistics; and a train/eval leakage audit (exact
and 5-gram overlap) *before* interpreting any gain.

## Standing failure-mode checklist

The recurring methodological commitments, stated once:

1. **Measure, don't assume.** The duality target is produced by stepping the
   weights and reversing the step; the activation metric is recorded from
   live activations; a batching optimization is admitted only with a proof of
   measurement invariance.
2. **Orthogonal instruments for confusable failures.** Adjoint vs range vs
   realization vs clipping; retrieval vs controller vs composition; canonical
   vs marginal vs dispersion. Never compress a verdict into one number.
3. **No proxy certificates.** Gate norm, clip fraction, and
   initial-tangent linearity are explicitly *not* duality certificates; the
   range residual is.
4. **Refuse, don't coerce.** Incompatible compositions, identity mismatches,
   and batches mixing different controllers are rejected, not silently fixed.
5. **Bound and disclose.** Every applied update is box-bounded, and the gap
   between the solved and the applied update is itself a reported quantity.

# Appendix: a metric for multiplicative-gate optimizers {.appendix #sec-appendix-metric}

A worked failure analysis, transferable to any setting (it was observed on
image-diffusion channel gates) where per-channel multiplicative log-gates $q$
are trained with an adaptive optimizer whose per-coordinate metric tracks
activation energy:

$$
M_t \;=\; M_{\mathrm{base}}\cdot\varphi(2q),
\qquad \text{post-step preconditioner } 1/\sqrt{M_t}.
$$

With $\varphi=\exp$: multiplicative gates *self-prune* unused channels
($q\to-\infty$ is a real signal worth keeping), but $\exp$ is asymmetric — as
$q\to-\infty$, $M_t\to0$ and the preconditioned step $u/\sqrt{M_t}\to\infty$,
so the optimizer **amplifies** negative drift instead of damping it (observed:
3–7% of channels running to $q\approx-40$). A naive clamp fails the opposite
way: thousands of finite tiny-metric dead coordinates dominate any global
metric-norm step cap and convert it into a global learning-rate divider. An
upper bound $q_{\max}$ cannot fix an unbounded lower tail.

The repaired scheme: (i) replace $\exp(2q)$ with $\cosh(2q)$, which damps
$|q|\to\infty$ symmetrically; (ii) represent pruned channels by a *finite*
floor $q_{\mathrm{prune}}$ (reference $-20$), with hard-dead channels given
forward scale exactly 0 and **excluded** from any global step-norm cap;
(iii) floor the metric, $M_{\mathrm{eff}}=\max(M, M_{\mathrm{floor}})$;
(iv) bound one-step damage with per-coordinate update caps, keeping global
caps off by default. Reported effect of the $\varphi$ swap on otherwise
identical runs: $\cosh$ keeps $q\in[-5.1,+3.8]$ with no channel beyond
$|q|>5$; $\exp$ runs to $q\approx-42$ with $\sim$20% beyond.

The general lesson matches @sec-protocol: a safety mechanism (the step cap)
must be audited under the failure it guards against, and an improvement that
appears only with hard pruning must be reported as structured pruning, not as
a linear-duality result.
